cases.title: Ocular Rosacea with Blepharitis
cases.presentation: 50 yo female with eye irritation OU
cases.photoDescription: Slit lamp photo shows collarettes surrounding eyelashes and prominent blood vessels

questions.question: What is your focused differential diagnosis?
questions.answer:
● Seborrheic Blepharitis
● Blepharitis 2/2 to rosacea
● Staphylococcal Blepharitis
● Pediculosis

questions.question: What questions do you want to ask the patient?
questions.answer:
● Itching, burning, FBS, tearing, crusting around eyes upon awakening
● Symptoms more associated with alcohol, coffee, tea consumption
keyPoints: Itching, burning, FBS, tearing, crusting around eyes upon awakening
Symptoms more associated with alcohol, coffee, tea consumption
scoringKeywords: Itching, burning, FBS, tearing, crusting around eyes upon awakening
Symptoms more associated with alcohol, coffee, tea consumption

questions.question: What exam findings will you look for?
questions.answer:
● Rosacea – telengiectasias, pustules, papules or erythema of cheeks, forehead, rhinophyma best seen in natural light
● Inspissated oil glands
● SPK
● Superficial or deep corneal vascularization esp inferior cornea
● Corneal infiltrates / phlyctenules
● Lice, nits and blood-tinged debris on eyelids and lashes
keyPoints: Rosacea – telengiectasias, pustules, papules or erythema of cheeks, forehead, rhinophyma best seen in natural light
Superficial or deep corneal vascularization esp inferior cornea
Lice, nits and blood-tinged debris on eyelids and lashes
scoringKeywords: Lice, nits and blood-tinged debris on eyelids and lashes
Superficial or deep corneal vascularization esp inferior cornea

questions.question: What tests will rule in or rule out your #1 treating diagnosis?
questions.answer: ● Not applicable

questions.question: How are you going to treat your #1 diagnosis?
questions.answer:
● Eyelid scrubs bid.
● Warm compresses bid.
● Erythromycin or bacitracin ointment qhs
● Unresponsive: Doxycycline 100mg PO bid
o Avoid in pregnant women, nursing mothers, children < 8 yrs
o Counsel regarding photosensitivity
● Associated dry eyes: preservative free artificial tears 4-8 times/day
● Short-term, low dose topical steroids for sterile corneal infiltrates or phlyctenulosis
● Rosacea: Facial lesions treated with metronidazole gel 0.75% BID
keyPoints: Associated dry eyes: preservative free artificial tears 4-8 times/day
Short-term, low dose topical steroids for sterile corneal infiltrates or phlyctenulosis
Rosacea: Facial lesions treated with metronidazole gel 0.75% BID

questions.question: What will you tell your patient about the natural history, prognosis and follow-up for treatment?
questions.answer:
Counsel the patient that this is a chronic condition that is controlled rather than cured. Avoid exacerbating foods, beverages,
environmental factors if rosacea. Follow-up is variable depending on severity of disease an involvement of cornea. Consider
dermatology referral for moderate to severe facial disease

cases.title: Chalazion
cases.presentation: 24 yo woman complaining of “stye” for weeks
cases.photoDescription: External photograph of the right eye showing well-circumscribed nodular lesion of the RLL with slightly erythematous overlying skin.

questions.question: What is your focused differential diagnosis?
questions.answer:
● Chalazion
● Hordeolum
● Preseptal Cellulitis
● Insect bite
● Pyogenic granuloma
● Sebaceous gland Carcinoma (in older patient with recurrent, unilateral chalazia w/ lash loss)
keyPoints: Sebaceous gland Carcinoma (in older patient with recurrent, unilateral chalazia w/ lash loss)

questions.question: What questions do you want to ask the patient?
questions.answer:
● Duration of symptoms
● Pain or tenderness
● History of sinusitis, local skin abrasions, insect bites, trauma
● Older patients – recurrent chalazia (concern for sebaceous gland carcinoma)
keyPoints: History of sinusitis, local skin abrasions, insect bites, trauma
Older patients – recurrent chalazia (concern for sebaceous gland carcinoma)
scoringKeywords: History of sinusitis, local skin abrasions, insect bites, trauma

questions.question: What exam findings will you look for?
questions.answer:
● Palpate for consistency and borders
● Evaluate for blepharitis – inspissated meibomian glands
● Evaluate for rosacea
● Eyelid swelling, erythema, warmth
● Evert lids to rule out other etiologies.
keyPoints: Evaluate for blepharitis – inspissated meibomian glands
Evert lids to rule out other etiologies.
scoringKeywords: Evert lids to rule out other etiologies.

questions.question: What tests will rule in or rule out your #1 treating diagnosis?
questions.answer: ● Not applicable

questions.question: How are you going to treat your #1 diagnosis?
questions.answer:
● Warm compresses for 10min qid with light massage over lesion.
● If lesion draining or associated with blepharitis: erythromycin or bacitracin ointment bid.
● Doxycycline 100mg bid if associated w/ blepharitis/MGD
● If chalazion fails to resolve after 3-4 weeks of appropriate medical therapy, incision and curettage vs. injection of steroid (if
lesion near lacrimal apparatus).
o Steroid injection: risk of skin depigmentation, CRAO
● Send recurrent or unusual chalazia to pathology

questions.question: What will you tell your patient about the natural history, prognosis and follow-up for treatment?
questions.answer:
Good prognosis. Lesion usually resolves over weeks to months when the contents drain either externally through eyelid skin or
internally through tarsus. Small amount of scar tissue may remain. Treating underlying blepharitis and MGD will decrease
recurrences.

cases.title: Neurotrophic Keratopathy
cases.presentation:
50 yo female complaining of foreign body sensation
and decreased vision
cases.photoDescription: Slit lamp photo shows a paracentral corneal ulcer highlighted by flourescein staining. The ulcer is horizontally oval and has smooth, well-def ined edges with surrounding corneal edema. There is no infiltrate

questions.question: What is your focused differential diagnosis?
questions.answer:
● Neurotrophic Keratopathy
● Sterile corneal melt 2/2 to NSAIDS
● HSV/HZV Keratitis – usually geographic ulcer
● Exposure Keratopathy
● Corneal Abrasion
● Topical anesthetic abuse

questions.question: What questions do you want to ask the patient?
questions.answer:
● Hx of HSV/HZV, Dm, stroke, tumor (especially acoustic neuroma)
● Previous irradiation to eye or adnexal structure
● Any other eye surgery
● Surgery near CN V distribution
● Hx of eye drops (diclofenac, proparacaine)
● Hx of crack cocaine use
● Hx of trauma
keyPoints: Hx of HSV/HZV, Dm, stroke, tumor (especially acoustic neuroma)
Previous irradiation to eye or adnexal structure
scoringKeywords: Previous irradiation to eye or adnexal structure
Hx of HSV/HZV, Dm, stroke, tumor

questions.question: What exam findings will you look for?
questions.answer:
● Check corneal sensation prior to anesthesia
● Look for corneal dendrites, stromal scarring
● Skin lesions
● Looks for CN abnormalities, esp CN VII, VIII => lagophthalmos, acoustic neuromas
keyPoints: Check corneal sensation prior to anesthesia
Look for corneal dendrites, stromal scarring
Looks for CN abnormalities, esp CN VII, VIII => lagophthalmos, acoustic neuromas
scoringKeywords: Look for corneal dendrites, stromal scarring
Check corneal sensation prior to anesthesia

questions.question: What tests will rule in or rule out your #1 treating diagnosis?
questions.answer: ● Not applicable

questions.question: How are you going to treat your #1 diagnosis?
questions.answer:
● Mild- Mod. PEE: AT q2-4H, ointment and consider plugs
● Epithelial defect: antibiotic ointment (erythromycin or bacitracin) q2 hours
● Ulceration: Bandage CL, Amniotic membrane, tarsorrhapy
● Work-up for bacterial keratitis if infiltrate present
keyPoints: Mild- Mod. PEE: AT q2-4H, ointment and consider plugs
Epithelial defect: antibiotic ointment (erythromycin or bacitracin) q2 hours
Ulceration: Bandage CL, Amniotic membrane, tarsorrhapy
Work-up for bacterial keratitis if infiltrate present
scoringKeywords: Work-up for bacterial keratitis if infiltrate present

questions.question: What will you tell your patient about the natural history, prognosis and follow-up for treatment?
questions.answer:
Counsel this patient that the cornea has lost sensation and has poor healing. This is a chronic problem. Acutely, the prognosis is
guarded as the epithelial defect can be slow to resolve and will need close follow-up to monitor for improvement.
F/u every 1-2 days until improvement then every 3-5 days until resolution.

cases.title: Interstitial Keratitis
cases.presentation: 18 yo male with blurred vision and pain in both eyes
cases.photoDescription: This slit lamp photo shows diffuse peripheral corneal neovascularization and corneal haze

questions.question: What is your focused differential diagnosis?
questions.answer:
● Interstitial keratitis from syphillis
● TB
● Lyme
● HSV/HZV/EBV
● Cogan syndrome (Tinnitus, vertigo, deafness)

questions.question: What questions do you want to ask the patient?
questions.answer:
● Hx of syphilis in patient or mother during pregnancy
● Hx of TB or symptoms
● Hx of tick bite
● Hx of HSV or HZV
● Hearing loss or tinnitus
keyPoints: Hx of syphilis in patient or mother during pregnancy
scoringKeywords: Hx of syphilis in patient or mother during pregnancy

questions.question: What exam findings will you look for?
questions.answer:
● Saddle-nose deformity, Hutchinson teeth, frontal bossing
● Check corneal sensation
● Cell/flare
● Cataracts
● Salt-and-pepper fundus or optic atrophy
● Enlarging skin rash (Lyme)
keyPoints: Saddle-nose deformity, Hutchinson teeth, frontal bossing
scoringKeywords: Saddle-nose deformity, Hutchinson teeth, frontal bossing

questions.question: What tests will rule in or rule out your #1 treating diagnosis?
questions.answer:
● RPR, FTA-ABS
● PPD or quantiferon
● CXR if neg FTA-ABS or positive PPD
● Lyme and EBV titers
keyPoints: CXR if neg FTA-ABS or positive PPD
scoringKeywords: CXR if neg FTA-ABS or positive PPD

questions.question: How are you going to treat your #1 diagnosis?
questions.answer:
● Acute disease- topical cycloplegic and steroid (q2h depending on severity)
● Treat underlying disease – syphilis, TB, Lyme
● Scarring from old inactive disease – corneal transplant if minimal amblyopia
keyPoints: Acute disease- topical cycloplegic and steroid (q2h depending on severity)
Treat underlying disease – syphilis, TB, Lyme
Scarring from old inactive disease – corneal transplant if minimal amblyopia
scoringKeywords: Acute disease- topical cycloplegic and steroid

questions.question: What will you tell your patient about the natural history, prognosis and follow-up for treatment?
questions.answer:
Prognosis is good with treatment however if scarring involves visual axis will require corneal transplant with intensive, long-term
follow-up
In the acute stage, follow-up the patient every 3-7 days initially then 2-4 weeks. Slowly taper the steroid (months to years) and closely
monitor IOP. For chronic, inactive disease: yearly follow-up unless treatment necessary

cases.title: Chemical Burns
cases.presentation:
24 yo male complaining of pain in left eye x 2 hours
after reporting something splashed into his eye
cases.photoDescription: This external photo shows a large corneal epithelial defect from limbus to limbus. There appears to be some peri-limbal ischemia with chemosis. The cornea is otherwise clear and the anterior chamber looks quiet ** Prior to obtaining more history => copious irrigation with saline or lactated ringers with a morgan lens if available. Check pH periodically until the pH is neutral. Also sweep the conjunctival fornices to remove any sequestered particles

questions.question: What is your focused differential diagnosis?
questions.answer:
● Chemical burn
● Corneal abrasion

questions.question: What questions do you want to ask the patient?
questions.answer:
● Time of injury
● Specific type of chemical
● Time between exposure until irrigation
● Eye protection
● Duration and type of irrigation

questions.question: What exam findings will you look for?
questions.answer:
● IOP
● Evert eyelids to search for foreign bodies
● Fluoroscein staining of cornea and conjunctiva
● Conjunctival and scleral ischemia or necrosis
● Corneal epi defect, edema, ulceration
● AC reaction
● Necrotic retinopathy
keyPoints: Evert eyelids to search for foreign bodies
Fluoroscein staining of cornea and conjunctiva
Conjunctival and scleral ischemia or necrosis
scoringKeywords: Fluoroscein staining of cornea and conjunctiva
Conjunctival and scleral ischemia or necrosis
Evert eyelids to search for foreign bodies

questions.question: What tests will rule in or rule out your #1 treating diagnosis?
questions.answer: ● See above => pH

questions.question: How are you going to treat your #1 diagnosis?
questions.answer:
● Copious irrigation to neutralize pH, cycloplegic (avoid phenylephrine 2/2 vasoconstriction), frequent artificial tears/gels,
topical erythromycin q1h while awake, oral pain control, oral or topical IOP control
● Significant inflammation without risk of corneal melting: Topical steroids (Tobradex ointment q2hrs)
● Consider amniotic membrane graft or Prokera
● Lysis of conjunctival adhesions vs symblepharon ring
● Corneal melting: Vit C, doxycycline, acetylcysteine
● Progressive melting or perforation: glue, patch graft, PK
keyPoints: Copious irrigation to neutralize pH, cycloplegic (avoid phenylephrine 2/2 vasoconstriction), frequent artificial tears/gels,
Significant inflammation without risk of corneal melting: Topical steroids (Tobradex ointment q2hrs)
Consider amniotic membrane graft or Prokera
Lysis of conjunctival adhesions vs symblepharon ring
Corneal melting: Vit C, doxycycline, acetylcysteine
Progressive melting or perforation: glue, patch graft, PK
scoringKeywords: Consider amniotic membrane graft or Prokera
Copious irrigation to neutralize pH, cycloplegic
Lysis of conjunctival adhesions vs symblepharon ring
Significant inflammation without risk of corneal melting

questions.question: What will you tell your patient about the natural history, prognosis and follow-up for treatment?
questions.answer:
Counsel this patient that he suffered a very serious injury to his eye and requires close follow up. Follow up with this patient daily,
checking the epithelial defect, IOP, and AC inflammation, tapering the steroids if used when inflammation has resolved.
Prognosis depends on severity of injury. Severe injuries can lead to corneal scarring and long-term ocular surface and glaucoma
issues.

cases.title: Keratoconjunctivitis sicca
cases.presentation: 44 yo F complains of burning eye pain OU at night
cases.photoDescription: This is a slit lamp photo of a fluorescein stain of the cornea which shows diffuse superficial punctate keratopathy.

questions.question: What is your focused differential diagnosis?
questions.answer:
● Dry eye syndrome
● Blepharitis
● CTL related disorder – Tight lens syndrome, CTL overwear
● Exposure keratopathy
● Topical medication toxicity
keyPoints: CTL related disorder – Tight lens syndrome, CTL overwear

questions.question: What questions do you want to ask the patient?
questions.answer:
● Contact lens wear?
● Eye drops?
● Hx of connective tissue Dz – RA, Lupus, Sjogrens
● Medications – antihistamines, beta-blockers, oral contraceptives
● Hx of Gastric bypass / Vitamin A deficiency
keyPoints: Hx of connective tissue Dz – RA, Lupus, Sjogrens
Hx of Gastric bypass / Vitamin A deficiency
scoringKeywords: Hx of Gastric bypass / Vitamin A deficiency

questions.question: What exam findings will you look for?
questions.answer:
● Slit lamp exam to look for eyelid/lash malpositions, meibomian glands
● Fluorescein exam to evaluate tear film, epithelial erosions
● Evert eyelids to look for foreign body, papillae or follicles
● Inspect contact lens and CTL fit
keyPoints: Slit lamp exam to look for eyelid/lash malpositions, meibomian glands
Fluorescein exam to evaluate tear film, epithelial erosions
Evert eyelids to look for foreign body, papillae or follicles
Inspect contact lens and CTL fit
scoringKeywords: Fluorescein exam to evaluate tear film, epithelial erosions
Slit lamp exam to look for eyelid/lash malpositions, meibomian glands
Inspect contact lens and CTL fit
Evert eyelids to look for foreign body, papillae or follicles

questions.question: What tests will rule in or rule out your #1 treating diagnosis?
questions.answer:
● Schirmer Test
● Consider Sjogrens antibodies (Anti-Ro, Anti-La)

questions.question: How are you going to treat your #1 diagnosis?
questions.answer:
● Mild: Nonpreserved artificial tears, lubricating ointment, omega 3 supplements
● Moderate/Severe: Topical cyclosporine, punctual plugs/cautery, autologous tears, scleral lenses
● IF CTL wearer d/c contact lens use
● If history suggests presence of connective tissue Dz (dry mouth, joint pain) refer to internist or rheumatologist for systemic
evaluation
keyPoints: Mild: Nonpreserved artificial tears, lubricating ointment, omega 3 supplements
Moderate/Severe: Topical cyclosporine, punctual plugs/cautery, autologous tears, scleral lenses
IF CTL wearer d/c contact lens use
If history suggests presence of connective tissue Dz (dry mouth, joint pain) refer to internist or rheumatologist for systemic
scoringKeywords: If history suggests presence of connective tissue Dz
IF CTL wearer d/c contact lens use

questions.question: What will you tell your patient about the natural history, prognosis and follow-up for treatment?
questions.answer:
Prognosis is good for improvement in symptoms, but depends on the underlying etiology. Most causes are chronic and require
prolonged therapy. The patient should be followed-up in weeks to months depending on the severity of corneal disease and symptoms.
Patients with an underlying systemic disease should be followed more closely.

cases.title: Cornea Endothelial disease
cases.presentation: 55 yo female complains of blurry vision OU
cases.photoDescription: Slit lamp photo shows cornea guttata and mild corneal edema

questions.question: What is your focused differential diagnosis?
questions.answer:
● Fuchs Endothelial Dystrophy
● Pseudoguttae 2/2 previous trauma, intraocular inflammation, infection, toxins
● Herpetic disciform keratitis
● Chandlers syndrome (ICE syndrome – unilateral rather than bilateral)
keyPoints: Pseudoguttae 2/2 previous trauma, intraocular inflammation, infection, toxins
Chandlers syndrome (ICE syndrome – unilateral rather than bilateral)
scoringKeywords: Pseudoguttae 2/2 previous trauma, intraocular inflammation, infection, toxins

questions.question: What questions do you want to ask the patient?
questions.answer:
● Vision worse in AM?
● Family history of corneal disorder
● History of trauma, infection, inflammation

questions.question: What exam findings will you look for?
questions.answer:
● Central epithelial edema and bullae, subepithelial scars, stromal edema
● Folds in Descemet’s membrane
● Fine pigment dusting on the endothelium
keyPoints: Central epithelial edema and bullae, subepithelial scars, stromal edema
Fine pigment dusting on the endothelium
scoringKeywords: Fine pigment dusting on the endothelium
Central epithelial edema and bullae, subepithelial scars, stromal edema

questions.question: What tests will rule in or rule out your #1 treating diagnosis?
questions.answer:
● Pachymetry
● Specular microscopy – decreased endo cell count, variable endothelial size and shape, numerous dark areas
keyPoints: Specular microscopy – decreased endo cell count, variable endothelial size and shape, numerous dark areas

questions.question: How are you going to treat your #1 diagnosis?
questions.answer:
● Topical sodium chlrodie 5% drops qid and ointment qhs
● May use blowdryer qam
● Ruptured bullae: topical antibiotic, bandage contact lens, cyclopegic, artificial tears
● DSAEK if decreased visual acuity 2/2 corneal edema. PK if significant scarring present
keyPoints: Topical sodium chlrodie 5% drops qid and ointment qhs
Ruptured bullae: topical antibiotic, bandage contact lens, cyclopegic, artificial tears
DSAEK if decreased visual acuity 2/2 corneal edema. PK if significant scarring present
scoringKeywords: Topical sodium chlrodie 5% drops qid and ointment qhs

questions.question: What will you tell your patient about the natural history, prognosis and follow-up for treatment?
questions.answer:
Fuchs is a slowly progressive condition that may require corneal transplantation in the future. Prior to cataract surgery, patient
should be counseled about the increased likelihood of corneal edema and prolonged recovery. A thorough evaluation should be
performed to determine if a combined cataract and corneal transplant procedure is indicated
Follow- up every 3-12 months based on severity of symptoms.

cases.title: Recurrent Erosion Syndrome
cases.presentation: 46 yo female with episodes of pain, photophobia and tearing
cases.photoDescription: 1) Slit lamp photo demonstrating corneal epithelial defect, localized roughening of epithelium with fluorescein staining 2) Higher Mag showing dots and comma shaped opacities

questions.question: What is your focused differential diagnosis?
questions.answer:
● Recurrent Corneal erosion 2/2 to map-dot-fingerprint dystrophy
o Also seen in Reis-Buckler, Meesmann’s and stromal dystrophies
● Corneal Abrasion
● Neurotrophic ulcer
● HSV keratitis (geographic defect)
● Topical anesthetic abuse
keyPoints: Recurrent Corneal erosion 2/2 to map-dot-fingerprint dystrophy
scoringKeywords: Recurrent Corneal erosion 2/2 to map-dot-fingerprint dystrophy

questions.question: What questions do you want to ask the patient?
questions.answer:
● Previous episodes
● Time of day – (recurrent corneal erosion typically occur upon waking)
● Hx of prior corneal abrasion
● Hx of prior ocular surgery (refractive, corneal transplant, or cataract surgery)
● Hx of Dm
● Family Hx of corneal dystrophy
keyPoints: Time of day – (recurrent corneal erosion typically occur upon waking)
Hx of prior ocular surgery (refractive, corneal transplant, or cataract surgery)

questions.question: What exam findings will you look for?
questions.answer:
● Corneal epithelial microcysts or dots, fingerprint patterns, maplike lines (check both eyes)
● Look for negative staining with flourescein
keyPoints: Corneal epithelial microcysts or dots, fingerprint patterns, maplike lines (check both eyes)
Look for negative staining with flourescein
scoringKeywords: Corneal epithelial microcysts or dots, fingerprint patterns, maplike lines
Look for negative staining with flourescein

questions.question: What tests will rule in or rule out your #1 treating diagnosis?
questions.answer: ● Not applicable

questions.question: How are you going to treat your #1 diagnosis?
questions.answer:
● Debridement of loose epithelial tissue
● Antibiotic ointment or drop, cycloplegic if iritis present, consider pressure patch or bandage contact lens if defect is large
(unless CTL wearer)
● Oral analgesics for pain
● Maintenance: 1) frequent AT w/ night time ointment or Muro
● If recalcitrant 1) BSCL for 3-6 months 2) epithelial debridement with diamond burr 3) anterior stromal puncture if outside
visual axis 4) phototherapeutic keratectomy
keyPoints: Antibiotic ointment or drop, cycloplegic if iritis present, consider pressure patch or bandage contact lens if defect is large
Maintenance: 1) frequent AT w/ night time ointment or Muro
If recalcitrant 1) BSCL for 3-6 months 2) epithelial debridement with diamond burr 3) anterior stromal puncture if outside

questions.question: What will you tell your patient about the natural history, prognosis and follow-up for treatment?
questions.answer:
Follow up q1-2 days until healed, then every 1-3 months depending on symptoms. Counsel patient prognosis is very good with
appropriate treatment although some patients have recalcitrant recurrent erosions.

cases.title: Peripheral Ulcerative Keratitis
cases.presentation: 32 year old female with severe eye pain and decreased vision
cases.photoDescription: This is an external photo showing conjunctival injection and scarring around the periphery of the inferior cornea. There appears to be associated thinning and neovascularization of the peripheral cornea.

questions.question: What is your focused differential diagnosis?
questions.answer:
● Peripheral ulcerative keratitis secondary to a systemic disease (sclera usually involved)
● Mooren ulcer (Sclera usually NOT involved; Leading undermined edge; Dx of exclusion)
● Infectious keratitis
● Staphylococcal hypersensitivity/marginal keratitis- less likely because no limbal clearing
keyPoints: Peripheral ulcerative keratitis secondary to a systemic disease (sclera usually involved)
Mooren ulcer (Sclera usually NOT involved; Leading undermined edge; Dx of exclusion)
Staphylococcal hypersensitivity/marginal keratitis- less likely because no limbal clearing
scoringKeywords: Peripheral ulcerative keratitis secondary to a systemic disease

questions.question: What questions do you want to ask the patient?
questions.answer:
● Hx of Connective tissue Dz (RA, SLE, Wegener, PAN)
o Joint swelling/pain, rashes
● Exposure to Hep C, TB, syphilis
● Prior ocular surgery
keyPoints: Hx of Connective tissue Dz (RA, SLE, Wegener, PAN)
Exposure to Hep C, TB, syphilis
scoringKeywords: Exposure to Hep C, TB, syphilis

questions.question: What exam findings will you look for?
questions.answer:
● Corneal thinning, epithelial defect, infiltrate
● AC reaction
● Scleritis, scleral thinning, bluish hue in natural light
● Posterior exam- vitritis, retinochoroidal lesions
● Eyelids for meibomian gland disease/ blepharitis
keyPoints: Scleritis, scleral thinning, bluish hue in natural light
Eyelids for meibomian gland disease/ blepharitis
scoringKeywords: Scleritis, scleral thinning, bluish hue in natural light
Eyelids for meibomian gland disease/ blepharitis

questions.question: What tests will rule in or rule out your #1 treating diagnosis?
questions.answer:
● Serum ANA, ESR, RF, ANCA, Hep C serology,
● CBC, RPR, FTA-ABS, PPD, quantiferon, FBS (prior to starting PO Steroids)
● Cultures of any suspicious infiltrate
keyPoints: Serum ANA, ESR, RF, ANCA, Hep C serology,
CBC, RPR, FTA-ABS, PPD, quantiferon, FBS (prior to starting PO Steroids)
scoringKeywords: Serum ANA, ESR, RF, ANCA, Hep C serology,
CBC, RPR, FTA-ABS, PPD, quantiferon, FBS

questions.question: How are you going to treat your #1 diagnosis?
questions.answer:
● Antibiotic ointment q2 hours
● Cycloplegic for AC reaction or pain
● Doxycycline 100mg PO BID
● Topical cyclosporine
● Systemic steroids (Prednisone 1-2mg/kg PO QD) with H2 blocker after screening for TB, syphilis, pregnancy, stool guaiac
● Rheumatologist for Immunosuppressive (MTX, cyclophosphamide)
● Excision of adjacent conjunctiva
● Tissue glue vs patch graft if perforation
● Similar treatment for Mooren’s Ulcer except if Hep C assoc then Interferon treatment may help
keyPoints: Cycloplegic for AC reaction or pain
Systemic steroids (Prednisone 1-2mg/kg PO QD) with H2 blocker after screening for TB, syphilis, pregnancy, stool guaiac
Tissue glue vs patch graft if perforation
Similar treatment for Mooren’s Ulcer except if Hep C assoc then Interferon treatment may help
scoringKeywords: Tissue glue vs patch graft if perforation
Cycloplegic for AC reaction or pain

questions.question: What will you tell your patient about the natural history, prognosis and follow-up for treatment?
questions.answer:
Serious eye condition that will require daily follow up to monitor for thinning/perforation and infection. Recovery is a very long
process and may involve surgical procedures such as patching the thinning cornea with tissue glue or PK. Close follow-up with a
rheumatologist will also be required. Protective eye wear needed at all times as perforation with minor trauma possible

cases.title: Terrien Marginal Degeneration
cases.presentation: 32 year old male referred by his optometrist for “corneal growth”
cases.photoDescription: This is an external photo showing a quiet appearing eye with a superior pannus and leading edge of lipid. There appears to be peripheral th inning.

questions.question: What is your focused differential diagnosis?
questions.answer:
● Terrien marginal degeneration
● Staphylococcal hypersensitivity/marginal keratitis
● Limbal stem cell deficiency
● Furrow Degeneration (older patient; clear zone between senile arcus and limbus)
keyPoints: Furrow Degeneration (older patient; clear zone between senile arcus and limbus)

questions.question: What questions do you want to ask the patient?

● Hx of previous infection
● Hx of trauma
● Hx of chemical injury
● Hx of contact lens wear

questions.question: What exam findings will you look for?

● Corneal thinning, epithelial defect, infiltrate
● Associated scleritis
● Eyelids for meibomian gland disease/ blepharitis
keyPoints: Eyelids for meibomian gland disease/ blepharitis
scoringKeywords: Eyelids for meibomian gland disease/ blepharitis

questions.question: What tests will rule in or rule out your #1 treating diagnosis?
questions.answer: ● Not applicable

questions.question: How are you going to treat your #1 diagnosis?

the-rule astigmatism which is commonly irregular. Perforation may occur spontaneously or with minor trauma so protective eye
wear is recommended at all times.
Close follow up to monitor for corneal thinning

questions.question: What will you tell your patient about the natural history, prognosis and follow-up for treatment?

● IOP control with gtts and/or systemic agent
● Cycloplegics
● Topical steroids
● Urgent Cataract extraction in 1-2 days
keyPoints: IOP control with gtts and/or systemic agent
scoringKeywords: IOP control with gtts and/or systemic agent

questions.question: What will you tell your patient about the natural history, prognosis and follow-up for treatment?
questions.answer:
The eye pressure usually does not respond adequately to medical therapy alone and patient will need urgent cataract extraction.
The prognosis for vision can be good but it depends on the amount of damage to the optic nerve as well as the amount of inflammation
and pressure. If there is no APD, would expect with good control of pressure and inflammation that his vision would likely improve
after cataract extraction.
Patient should be seen at least daily, with the consideration of hospitalization, to monitor pressures until the cataract is removed AND
the IOP normalizes

questions.question: What will you tell your patient about the natural history, prognosis and follow-up for treatment?
questions.answer:
The natural history depends on the affected parts of the eye. The prognosis is poor in the presence of nystagmus. Patient may need
genetic counseling with coordination with the primary doctor for possible further work-up for other abnormalities such as those found
in CHARGE syndrome. Follow-up will depend on associated refractive error, nystagmus or amblyopia

questions.question: What tests will rule in or rule out your #1 treating diagnosis?
questions.answer:
● culture and sensitivity of any external purulent material encountered for grade 1 and 2. Vitreous cultures for grade 3.
o early: staph epi/staph aureous
o months - years: strep, haemophilus, s. aureous, moraxells, pseudomonas, serratia
keyPoints: culture and sensitivity of any external purulent material encountered for grade 1 and 2. Vitreous cultures for grade 3.

questions.question: How are you going to treat your #1 diagnosis?
questions.answer:
● Grade 1: Hourly fortified antibiotics (vancomycin + tobramycin) or FQ q1hr; start steroids 24 hrs after abx and blebitis
resolving
● Grade 2: add a cycloplegic, consider oral FQ - more careful monitoring
● Grade 3: As in endophthalmitis
o HM or better: Tap and inject using intravitreal vancomycin + ceftazidime. Send for gram stain, culture, sensitivities.
Consider intravitreal steroids in select cases w/ severe vitreous inflammation
o LP or worse/worsening despite aggressive therapy: Vitrectomy with vitreous biopsy for cultures and gram/giemsa
stains.
keyPoints: Grade 1: Hourly fortified antibiotics (vancomycin + tobramycin) or FQ q1hr; start steroids 24 hrs after abx and blebitis
Grade 2: add a cycloplegic, consider oral FQ - more careful monitoring

questions.question: What will you tell your patient about the natural history, prognosis and follow-up for treatment?

Follow the patient q12 - 24 hours until improvement is evident. Inform the patient of a guarded prognosis as she is at risk for losing
vision permanently. However, with timely management good outcomes are possible.

questions.question: How are you going to treat your #1 diagnosis?
questions.answer:
● Protect the eyes from sun, dust and wind.
● Lubrication- AT 4-8 x daily
● If inflammed: mod-severe- mild topical steroid (loteprednol QID) vs. NSAID (Acular BID-QID) with taper
● If delle: lubricating ointment q2H
● Surgery if progressing toward visual axis, no relief of symptoms with treatment, pt unable to wear CL due to lesion
keyPoints: Protect the eyes from sun, dust and wind.
If inflammed: mod-severe- mild topical steroid (loteprednol QID) vs. NSAID (Acular BID-QID) with taper

questions.question: What will you tell your patient about the natural history, prognosis and follow-up for treatment?
questions.answer:
● Prognosis is good to very good depending on severity. Pterygium can continue to grow and cause increased dryness,
irritation and even decreased vision. Pterygium can recur after removal, occasionally worse than the original pterygium.
● If symptomatic and initiating treatment then follow-up every 1-3 months depending on severity and whether topical steroids
are used, otherwise every 6-12 months to monitor for progression.

questions.question: What will you tell your patient about the natural history, prognosis and follow-up for treatment?
questions.answer:
● Schnyder dz – Can recur in graft; Lipid management does not affect progression of dystrophy
● Cystinosis – can recur in graft; life expectancy is normal in adult form
● Bietti – corneal crystals may fade with time however retinal crystals may progress to RPE atrophy and choroidal sclerosis
leading to nyctalopia, poor dark adaptation, central and VF loss; family members should be evaluated
● Lymphoproliferative disorder – crystals usually resolve with treatment of underlying malignancy
● FQ Drops – crystals resolve with cessation of drops

questions.question: What exam findings will you look for?

questions.question: How are you going to treat your #1 diagnosis?

questions.question: What will you tell your patient about the natural history, prognosis and follow-up for treatment?
questions.answer:

silicone intubation for chronic or recurrent dacryocystitis.

cases.title: Quantify low vision
cases.presentation: Quantify low vision

questions.question: Discussion
questions.answer:
Low vision is when a patient’s visual needs exceed his or her visual capabilities. This may occur with a VA of 20/40 in one patient
and 20/200 in another. As such, there are no set measurements that quantify low vision as in legal blindness.
The low vision examination consists of history, examination and a trial of variety of low vision aids. The history determines the
patient’s visual and functional goals in regards to attention to daily living, occupation, or hobbies. The examination should include
measuring visual acuity with a low vision chart or by moving the patient closer to the chart. Both distance and near visual acuity
should be measured. Other findings that may influence the type of aid prescribed should be noted such as other physical disabilities,
nystagmus, photophobia, aniridia, etc. An optimal refraction should be obtained using Kestenbaum’s Rule to estimate the strength of a
near add.

cases.title: What needs to be considered for IOL calculations post-refractive surgery?
cases.presentation: What needs to be considered for IOL calculations post-refractive surgery?

questions.question: Discussion
questions.answer:
Keratometry assumes a normal relationship between the anterior and posterior corneal curvatures and measure the anterior corneal
radius. Incisional keratorefractive surgery for myopia flattens both the anterior and posterior corneal radius. Ablative keratorefractive
surgery for myopia flattens the anterior corneal radius but leaves the posterior corneal radius mostly unchanged. Standard
keratometry extrapolates the central corneal power based on paracentral measurements but in these settings will over-
estimate the central corneal power leading to postoperative hyperopic surprise.
In addition, if the central power is very low, many formulas assume the anterior chamber is shallow and the IOL will sit closer to the
cornea than normal and call for less power. The Holladay 2 formula and Double K correction method with SRK/T, Hoffer Q or
Holladay 1 formulas can compensate for this error.

cases.title: How is accommodative amplitude measured?
cases.presentation: How is accommodative amplitude measured?

questions.question: Discussion
questions.answer:
One eye is occluded with the best distance correction in place. Slowly move a target closer to the eye while strongly encouraging the
patient to keep it in focus. The target is slid along a Prince rule or an RAF (royal air force) rule. Record the point at which the patient
is no longer able to keep the target in focus. Repeat for the other eye. The dioptric value of the NPA is then equal to the
accommodative amplitude.
For presbyopes, test with a +3.00 add then subtract 3D from accommodative amplitude measured

cases.title: Transposing plus to minus cylinder
cases.presentation: Transposing plus to minus cylinder

questions.question: Discussion
questions.answer:
Transpose the following: +2.50 + 0.75 x 55
In order to transpose from plus to minus cylinder you must:
1) Add the sphere and cylinder together
(+2.50 +0.75= +3.25)
2) Change the sign of the cylinder (+0.75 🡪 - 0.75)
3) Add 90 to the axis (55 +90 = 145)
Now put it together! +3.25 – 0.75 x 145

cases.title: Dealing with a patient who is not accepting spectacle correction of astigmatism?
cases.presentation: Dealing with a patient who is not accepting spectacle correction of astigmatism?

questions.question: Discussion
questions.answer:
- Always re-refract and ensure the patient has the correct refraction (pay attention to significant anisometropia)
- Check if ordered glasses were correctly made (Rx, placement of optical centers, base curves of new glasses match old
glasses, minus cylinder ground on to back of lens)
- Minimize vertex distance to decrease magnification
- Reassure the patient that it may take time to adjust to new prescription
- Note if the cylinders are at oblique angles to one another
- Decrease the power of the cylinder while keeping S.E. constant
- Rotate the cylinder towards 90 or 180 meridian
- Discuss CL or refractive surgery when appropriate

cases.title: How do you assess the fit of an RGP lens? Soft lens?
cases.presentation: How do you assess the fit of an RGP lens? Soft lens?

questions.question: Discussion
questions.answer:
Basic Evaluation
● refract the patient, get the sphere in minus cylinder (use sphere only unless fitting toric lens)
● correct this value for vertex distance
● evaluate the patient’s corneal health
● obtain keratometry
RGP LENS (better for patients with high ametropia, astigmatism, corneal irregularities / less comfortable)
● First lens choice should have base curve 0.5D plus the flattest K reading
● Fluorescein test
o Slight apical clearing, mid peripheral touch with slight edge clearance => Good Fit
o Apical touch with peripheral => too loose
▪ choose a steeper base curve (smaller radius of curvature) or larger lens diameter
o Apical clearance with central pooling, bubbles => too tight
▪ choose a flatter base curve (larger radius of curvature) or smaller lens diameter
● Remember SAM/FAP rule if you change base curve
o Steeper add Minus to power
o Flatter add Plus to power
SOFT LENS
▪ Movement on Blink: CTL should move 1mm down with blink and reposition
o too much movement, it is too loose (flat)
o too little, too tight (steep)
▪ Centration: should cover entire cornea
▪ Sag: lens should drop 0.5 to 1 mm on upgaze
▪ Toric: check lens rotation and stability

questions.question: Discussion
questions.answer:
From bag to sulcus

cases.title: What is chromatic aberration & duochrome test?
cases.presentation: What is chromatic aberration & duochrome test?

questions.question: Discussion
questions.answer:
Chromatic aberration
When polychromatic light passes through a lens, shorter wavelengths (blue) are refracted (bent) more than longer wavelengths
(red) so that the former comes in focus closer to the lens than the latter. This is caused by the prismatic effect of lenses.
Duochrome test
This principle is applied in the duochrome test used to refine the spherical endpoint after performing manifest refraction. It is a
monocular test (done with each eye separately). First, the eye is fogged by adding +0.5 D to relax accommodation. A chart is shown
with black letters on a half-red and half-green background. The letters on the red side should be in sharper focus first due to fogging.
Then, minus sphere is dialed in until the letters are equally clear on the red and green sides.
“RAM GAP” - Red add minus, green add plus
There is a 1.0 D difference between the red and green light-rays. The duochrome test results in the image being in best focus on the
retina for white light conditions.
Does this test work on color blind individuals?
Yes, because it relies on chromatic aberration, not on color discrimination.
What range of VA is required to perform the duochrome test?
20/30 or better

cases.title: What is a Geneva lens clock?
cases.presentation: What is a Geneva lens clock?

questions.question: Discussion
questions.answer:
Measures the radius of curvature of a lens surface by the deflection of a movable pin. Base curves affect the magnification of the lens
independent of the lens power. If a patient is accustomed to a certain base curve (magnification), a change in base curve - with
constant overall dioptric power – may cause asthenopic symptoms.

cases.title: What is a lensmeter?
cases.presentation: What is a lens meter?

questions.question: Discussion
questions.answer:
A lens meter is used to measure sphere, cylinder, axis, prism and locate the optical center of a lens
It works based on the optometer principle and is made of an illuminated movable target behind a fixed lens, and a place between the
fixed lens and telescope to position the unknown lens. The target is moved to determine the focal length and thus the power of the
unknown lens.
How to use the instrument:
1. Rotate the axis dial until the narrow lines are all parallel
2. Rotate the drum until the narrow lines are in focus. This is sphere
3. Rotate the drum until the solid lines are in focus. Should be in positive direction otherwise rotate axis 90 degrees.
For prism: Make sure lens is centered and location of center of target from center cross hairs is noted. Each concentric circle away
from center equals 1 PD. Nasal= Base-in, Temporal= Base-out, Superior = Base up, Inferior = Base down

cases.title: Unhappy patient with current glasses: what do you do?
cases.presentation: Unhappy patient with current glasses: what do you do?

questions.question: Discussion
questions.answer:
Take a detailed history to find out:
● Symptoms: blurred vision, distorted vision, anisokonia, asthenopia, diploplia
● Setting: Difficulty with distance reading? near reading? Bifocal segment? Night-time? Glare?
● Old Mrx better: have patient bring in old glasses to compare
Evaluation
● Correct Mrx – repeat refraction, cycloplegic if concern for over-minused
● Correct glasses: Does not match Mrx, astigmatism axis wrong/power too strong
● Amount and axis of cylinder – rotate closer to 90 or 180 degrees
● Mechanical
o Check fit
o Check optical centers (unintentional prism)
o Check vertex distance (may be different from old glasses)
o Check base curve using lens clock (if different then previous lenses can change magnification)
● Bifocals: style of add changed, segment placement, progressive Rx not strong enough requiring patient to look too low,
● Induced prism from anisometropia – slab off prism, CL, lower both optical centers, single vision glasses, dissimilar segments,
Fresnel prism over bifocal segment
● Night vision issues: Pt may over accommodate at night due to no target and induce myopia. Add -0.25D
● Ocular pathology: surgically induced anisometropia(Usually < 2 D), ocular surface, cataract, retina finding
● Check for phoria or tropia

cases.title: Explain Spherical aberration
cases.presentation: Explain Spherical aberration

questions.question: Discussion
questions.answer:
Rays that strike the periphery of a lens system will be more strongly bent (if positive spherical aberration is present), effectively
shortening its focal length. This becomes a problem with widely dilated pupils (pharmacologic or in low light levels) causing the
patient to become more myopic,
This explains the phenomenon in retinoscopy when you have against movement in periphery and with movement centrally. Peripheral
lens refracts light more strongly than central rays so periphery appears more myopic. One should neutralize central reflex.

cases.title: Explain critical angle, total internal reflection and its application to ophthalmology
cases.presentation: Explain critical angle, total internal reflection and its application to ophthalmology

questions.question: Discussion
questions.answer:
CRITICAL ANGLE is the angle at which light is bent exactly 90 degress away from the normal causing light to run parallel with the
refractive interface. When the angle of incidence exceeds the critical angle the light is reflected back into the material with the higher
index of refraction causing TOTAL INTERNAL REFRACTION. Light leaving the angle (i.e trabecular meshwork) is reflected back
into the anterior chamber at the tear-air interface bacause of total internal reflection. Gonioscopy overcomes this by eliminating the
tear-air interface.

cases.title: Explain the prismatic effect of glasses on strabismic deviations
cases.presentation: Explain the prismatic effect of glasses on strabismic deviations

questions.question: Discussion
questions.answer:
Plus lenses decrease the measured deviation, whether ET, XT or HT
Minus Lenses increase the measured deviation, whether ET, XT or HT
The true deviation is changed by approximately (2.5) (D) % where D = bilateral spectacle lens power
For example an exotrope of 40 prism D wearing -10.00 sphere glasses will measure:
(2.5) (10) = 25% more XT for a total of 50 prism D of XT
3M mnemonic: Minus Measures More

cases.title: Bifocal Segments- prismatic effects (image jump vs. image displacement)
cases.presentation: Bifocal Segments- prismatic effects (image jump vs. image displacement)

questions.question: Discussion
questions.answer:
Image jump – due to sudden introduction of prismatic power at top of bifocal segment. With round top segments, sudden introduction
of base down prism causes an object to “jump” upward as the eye moves down. If optical center of bifocal is at top of segment (as in
Franklin or Executive type) or progressives, there is no image jump.
Image displacement – produced by total prismatic power acting in the reading position. Minimized when prismatic effect of bifocal is
opposite to that of the distance segment.
Myopic patients should be given flat top segments (Franklin/Executive style). This minimizes both image jump and image
displacement.
Hyperopic patients – round tops minimize image displacement and maximize image jump; flat top or Franklin-type segments
minimize image jump while maximizing image displacement.
Image jump is more bothersome than image displacement. In practice, many opticians give flat top segments to myopes and hyperopes
because flat top segments are easier and cheaper to assemble. As long as a patient is not complaining, this is acceptable.

cases.title: Patient complains that glasses fog and get dirty.
cases.presentation: Patient complains that glasses fog and get dirty

questions.question: Discussion
questions.answer:
Tight Frames (poor air circulation): adjust tilt to minimize contact and increase vertex distance.
Large frames (rub the eyebrows or rest on the cheeks): change to a smaller frame size.
Glass frames: change to plastic lenses which conduct heat more poorly than glass.
Treat underlying eye conditions:
- epiphora may cause fogging
- seborrheic dermatitis may promote deposition of dirt and oil on the lenses.

cases.title: Why would hyperopic patient slide glasses down his/her nose?
cases.presentation: Why would hyperopic patient slide glasses down his/her nose?

questions.question: Discussion
questions.answer:
Moving a lens, either plus or minus, away from the eye gives it more effective plus power since it pulls its far point with it. For
distance viewing this would help the patient if the plus lens prescribed is too weak. The patient can slide his glasses forward to adjust
for this error. Another reason is that near viewing can be enhanced by increasing the effective plus power.

cases.title: Describe the technique of streak retinoscopy.
cases.presentation: Describe the technique of streak retinoscopy

questions.question: Discussion
questions.answer:
Retinoscopy is a method of objectively measuring the refractive state of an eye. With a phoropter or free lenses, the examiner moves
the light streak across the pupil and changes the correcting lens until the reflex movement is neutralized (pupil appears uniformly
illuminated). The final refraction is determined by adjusting for working distance.
Myopic: far point between examiner and pupil => reflex moves in direction opposite to retinoscope sweep.
Hyperopic: far point behind examiner => reflex moves in same direction as retinoscope sweep.
